sun.name: The Sun

stats.sizeVsEarth: 109 Earths wide!

facts: The Sun is a giant ball of super-hot gas called a star. It's so hot that nothing solid could ever touch it!
If the Sun were a big beach ball, Earth would be the size of a tiny pea.
Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth. That means when you see sunshine, it actually left the Sun 8 minutes ago!
The Sun is so big that over a million Earths could fit inside it. That's like filling a house with a million marbles!
The Sun is about 4 and a half billion years old. That's way older than any dinosaur ever was!
Without the Sun, Earth would be a frozen dark ball floating in space. The Sun gives us light, heat, and makes plants grow!
The outside of the Sun is about 10,000 degrees. Your oven at home only goes to about 500 degrees!
The Sun is actually white in space. It looks yellow from Earth because our air changes the color a little bit.

mercury.name: Mercury

stats.sizeVsEarth: About 1/3 the size of Earth

facts: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun and the smallest planet in our solar system. It's only a little bigger than our Moon!
A year on Mercury is only 88 days. You'd have over 4 birthdays every Earth year!
Mercury has no air to breathe and no weather. There are no clouds, no rain, and no wind.
Even though Mercury is closest to the Sun, it's NOT the hottest planet. Venus is hotter because of its thick blanket of air!
During the day, Mercury is super hot — hot enough to melt a tin toy. At night, it's colder than any freezer on Earth!
Mercury is covered in craters from space rocks smashing into it, just like our Moon.
Mercury is so small and so close to the Sun that it's really hard to see in the sky. You can only spot it near sunrise or sunset.
If you weigh 70 pounds on Earth, you'd only weigh about 27 pounds on Mercury. You could jump really high!

venus.name: Venus

stats.sizeVsEarth: Almost the same size as Earth

facts: Venus is the hottest planet, even hotter than Mercury! Its thick clouds trap heat like a super-powered blanket.
Venus spins backwards compared to most planets. If you lived there, the Sun would rise in the west and set in the east!
A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It spins so slowly that one day takes 243 Earth days, but one trip around the Sun takes only 225!
Venus is sometimes called Earth's twin because they're almost the same size, but Venus is NOT a nice place to visit.
Venus is covered in thick yellow clouds made of acid. You can never see the ground from space!
Venus is the brightest planet in our sky. You can sometimes see it in the morning or evening and it looks like a super-bright star!
It rains acid on Venus! But the rain is so hot it turns to gas before it hits the ground.
The air on Venus is so heavy it would feel like being deep underwater. It would squish you flat!

earth.name: Earth

stats.sizeVsEarth: 1x (it IS Earth!)

facts: Earth is the only planet we know of that has life! From tiny bugs to giant whales, everything alive lives here.
About 70% of Earth is covered in water. That's why it looks blue from space and is sometimes called the Blue Marble!
If Earth were the size of a basketball, the Moon would be a tennis ball about 24 feet away.
Earth is spinning right now! It's turning at over 1,000 miles per hour, but we can't feel it because everything moves with us.
Earth has a giant magnet inside it. That's what makes a compass point north and protects us from the Sun's harmful rays!
Earth is the only planet NOT named after a Greek or Roman god. Its name comes from an old English word meaning 'the ground.'
The deepest part of the ocean is so deep you could stack 29 Empire State Buildings and they still wouldn't poke out of the water!
Earth is about 4 and a half billion years old. If Earth's life were one day, humans showed up in the last second before midnight!

mars.name: Mars

stats.sizeVsEarth: About half the size of Earth

facts: Mars is called the Red Planet because rusty iron in its dirt makes it look red, like a giant rusty playground!
Mars has the tallest mountain in the whole solar system! Olympus Mons is almost 3 times taller than Mount Everest.
A day on Mars is almost the same as a day on Earth — just 37 minutes longer. Perfect for extra playtime!
Mars has two tiny moons named Phobos and Deimos. They look more like lumpy potatoes than round balls!
We've sent robots called rovers to Mars! They drive around, take pictures, and study rocks for us.
Mars has ice caps at the top and bottom, just like Earth! They're made of frozen water and frozen carbon dioxide (dry ice).
Mars has a giant canyon called Valles Marineris that's so long it could stretch across the entire United States!
Scientists think Mars once had rivers and oceans billions of years ago. Maybe one day we'll find out if anything lived there!

jupiter.name: Jupiter

stats.sizeVsEarth: 11 Earths wide! (1,300 Earths could fit inside)

facts: Jupiter is the biggest planet in our solar system. It's so big that ALL the other planets could fit inside it with room to spare!
Jupiter has a giant storm called the Great Red Spot that's been spinning for over 300 years. It's bigger than Earth!
Jupiter spins faster than any other planet. Even though it's huge, one day is only about 10 hours long!
Jupiter has at least 95 moons! Four of them are really big and were discovered over 400 years ago by a man named Galileo.
Jupiter is a gas giant. It doesn't have solid ground to stand on — you'd just sink into thicker and thicker gas!
Jupiter is like a mini solar system with all its moons. Some of its moons are bigger than the planet Mercury!
Jupiter's stripes are actually bands of super-fast winds and swirling clouds going in different directions.
Jupiter acts like a space bodyguard for Earth! Its strong gravity pulls in space rocks that might otherwise head our way.

saturn.name: Saturn

stats.sizeVsEarth: 9 Earths wide!

facts: Saturn's rings are made of billions of pieces of ice and rock, some as tiny as grains of sand and some as big as houses!
Saturn is so light for its size that if you could find a bathtub big enough, Saturn would float in the water!
Saturn's rings stretch out really far but are super thin — like a DVD that's the size of a football field.
Saturn has over 140 moons! One of them, Titan, has lakes and rivers, but they're made of liquid natural gas, not water!
If you could drive a car to Saturn at highway speed, it would take over 1,000 years to get there!
Saturn's rings aren't solid — there are gaps between the rings. The biggest gap is called the Cassini Division.
Wind on Saturn can blow at over 1,000 miles per hour. That's way faster than the strongest hurricane on Earth!
Every 15 years, Saturn's rings seem to disappear when we see them edge-on because they're so thin!

uranus.name: Uranus

stats.sizeVsEarth: 4 Earths wide!

facts: Uranus rolls around the Sun on its side like a bowling ball! Scientists think something huge crashed into it long ago and knocked it over.
Uranus is an ice giant. Under its clouds, there might be an ocean of super-hot water, ammonia, and other icy stuff.
A year on Uranus is 84 Earth years. If you were born on Uranus, you wouldn't have your first birthday until you were 84 in Earth years!
Uranus looks blue-green because of a gas called methane in its atmosphere that soaks up red light.
Uranus is so far away that sunlight takes about 2 hours and 40 minutes to reach it!
Uranus has 13 faint rings, but they're very hard to see. They were only discovered in 1977!
Uranus was the first planet discovered using a telescope. A man named William Herschel found it in 1781.
Because Uranus is tilted on its side, its north pole gets 42 years of sunlight and then 42 years of darkness!

neptune.name: Neptune

stats.sizeVsEarth: About 4 Earths wide

facts: Neptune has the strongest winds of any planet — over 1,200 miles per hour! That's faster than the speed of sound!
Neptune is so far away that it takes over 4 hours for sunlight to reach it. It's a very dark, cold place.
A year on Neptune is 165 Earth years. Neptune has only completed one orbit since it was discovered in 1846!
Neptune is a beautiful deep blue color because of methane gas in its atmosphere. It's the prettiest shade of blue in the solar system!
Neptune was found using math before anyone ever saw it! Scientists noticed Uranus wobbling and calculated where Neptune must be.
Neptune has a moon called Triton that orbits backwards. Scientists think Neptune captured it from somewhere else in space!
Even though Neptune is far from the Sun, it gives off more heat than it receives. It has its own internal heater!
Neptune has very faint rings made of dust and ice, but they're almost impossible to see.

pluto.name: Pluto

stats.sizeVsEarth: About 1/6 the size of Earth

facts: Pluto is so far away that sunlight takes over 5 hours to reach it. At noon on Pluto, it's about as bright as twilight on Earth!
Pluto has a giant heart shape on its surface! It's made of frozen nitrogen ice and is called Tombaugh Regio.
Pluto used to be called the ninth planet, but in 2006 scientists reclassified it as a 'dwarf planet.' It's still special though!
A year on Pluto is 248 Earth years. Since Pluto was discovered in 1930, it hasn't even finished one trip around the Sun yet!
Pluto is smaller than Earth's Moon. It's tiny! But it still has five moons of its own.
Pluto and its biggest moon Charon are so close in size that they spin around each other like two dancers holding hands!
It's so cold on Pluto that the air itself freezes and falls to the ground like snow, then becomes air again when Pluto gets a tiny bit warmer.
Pluto was discovered by a 24-year-old astronomer named Clyde Tombaugh. An 11-year-old girl named Venetia suggested its name!

moon.name: The Moon

stats.sizeVsEarth: About 1/4 the size of Earth

facts: The Moon is the only place beyond Earth that humans have visited! Twelve astronauts have walked on it.
The Moon doesn't make its own light. It shines because sunlight bounces off it, like a giant mirror in the sky!
The Moon is slowly drifting away from Earth — about 1.5 inches every year. That's about as fast as your fingernails grow!
The dark spots on the Moon are called 'seas' but there's no water there at all! They're actually flat areas of old lava.
Footprints left by astronauts on the Moon will last for millions of years because there's no wind or rain to wash them away!
The same side of the Moon always faces Earth. The other side — the 'far side' — was a mystery until spacecraft flew around it!
You weigh much less on the Moon. If you can jump 1 foot on Earth, you could jump 6 feet on the Moon!
The Moon causes ocean tides on Earth! Its gravity pulls on our water, making it slosh back and forth every day.

phobos.name: Phobos

stats.sizeVsEarth: Tiny! Only about 14 miles across

facts: Phobos is one of the smallest moons in the solar system. It's shaped like a lumpy potato, not a smooth ball!
Phobos zooms around Mars so fast that it rises and sets THREE times in one Martian day!
Phobos is slowly getting closer to Mars. In about 50 million years, it will either crash into Mars or break into a ring!
Phobos has a huge crater called Stickney that's almost half as wide as the whole moon. The crash that made it nearly broke Phobos apart!
The name Phobos means 'fear' in Greek. It was named after the son of Ares, the Greek god of war.

deimos.name: Deimos

stats.sizeVsEarth: Even tinier! Only about 8 miles across

facts: Deimos is one of the smallest moons we know about. It's so small you could drive around it in a car in about an hour!
Deimos looks like a lumpy space rock because it's probably an asteroid that Mars captured with its gravity long ago.
The name Deimos means 'terror' in Greek. Pretty scary name for such a tiny little moon!
If you stood on Deimos and looked up, Mars would fill most of the sky! It would look enormous.
Deimos is slowly drifting away from Mars, the opposite of what its brother Phobos is doing.

io.name: Io

stats.sizeVsEarth: A bit bigger than Earth's Moon

facts: Io is the most volcanic place in the entire solar system! It has hundreds of volcanoes, and some are erupting right now!
Io's volcanoes shoot lava fountains hundreds of miles into space! That's higher than any rocket on Earth can fly.
Io looks like a pizza! Its surface is covered in yellow, red, orange, and brown splotches from all the sulfur its volcanoes spit out.
Jupiter's gravity squeezes and stretches Io like a stress ball, and all that squishing is what makes it so volcanic inside!
Io's surface is always changing because new lava keeps covering up the old craters. It's one of the youngest surfaces in the solar system!

europa.name: Europa

stats.sizeVsEarth: A bit smaller than Earth's Moon

facts: Europa has a giant ocean of liquid water hiding under a thick shell of ice! There might be more water there than in all of Earth's oceans!
Scientists think Europa might be one of the best places to look for alien life in our solar system because of its hidden ocean!
Europa's icy surface is covered in cracks and lines that make it look like a cracked egg. The ice is always shifting and breaking!
Europa is super smooth. It's one of the smoothest objects in the solar system, with very few craters or mountains.
The ice on Europa might be 10 to 15 miles thick. That's way thicker than any ice on Earth!

ganymede.name: Ganymede

stats.sizeVsEarth: Bigger than the planet Mercury!

facts: Ganymede is the biggest moon in the whole solar system! It's even bigger than the planet Mercury!
Ganymede is the only moon that has its own magnetic field, like a tiny invisible shield around it!
If Ganymede orbited the Sun instead of Jupiter, we'd probably call it a planet. It's that big!
Ganymede might also have a salty ocean hiding deep under its icy surface, like its neighbor Europa.
Ganymede has two types of surface: old, dark, cratered areas and newer, lighter, grooved areas. It's like looking at two different worlds!

callisto.name: Callisto

stats.sizeVsEarth: Almost as big as Mercury

facts: Callisto is the most cratered object in the solar system! Its surface is completely covered in dents from space rocks hitting it.
Callisto is the farthest of Jupiter's four big moons. It orbits so far out that Jupiter's squishing gravity doesn't bother it much.
Callisto has barely changed in 4 billion years. While its neighbor Io is covered in fresh lava, Callisto's craters just sit there untouched!
Callisto might have a small ocean of salty water hiding under its surface too. Jupiter's big moons love hiding oceans!
Scientists think Callisto would be a good place to build a base for exploring Jupiter someday because it has less harmful radiation than the other big moons.

titan.name: Titan

stats.sizeVsEarth: Bigger than Mercury! About 40% the size of Earth

facts: Titan is the only moon in the solar system with a thick atmosphere! You can't even see its surface through the orange hazy clouds.
Titan has lakes, rivers, and rain — but it's not water! It's liquid methane and ethane, which are like natural gas on Earth.
If you stood on Titan, the air is thick enough and the gravity is low enough that you could strap on wings and fly by flapping your arms!
Titan is so cold that water ice is as hard as rock there. The 'rocks' on Titan's surface are actually chunks of ice!
A space probe called Huygens landed on Titan in 2005. It's the farthest landing from Earth any spacecraft has ever made!
Titan is bigger than the planet Mercury, making it the second-largest moon in the solar system after Ganymede.

enceladus.name: Enceladus

stats.sizeVsEarth: Tiny! It could fit inside the state of Arizona

facts: Enceladus shoots giant fountains of water ice into space from cracks near its south pole! They're like huge geysers!
Under its icy shell, Enceladus has a warm salty ocean. Scientists think it might be one of the best places to look for tiny alien life!
Enceladus is one of the shiniest objects in the solar system because it's covered in fresh, bright white ice and snow.
The water that Enceladus sprays into space actually helps make one of Saturn's rings! The E ring is fed by Enceladus's geysers.
Enceladus is small but mighty. Even though it's only about 310 miles across, it has features you'd expect on much bigger worlds!

titania.name: Titania

stats.sizeVsEarth: Less than half the size of Earth's Moon

facts: Titania is the largest moon of Uranus and the eighth-largest moon in our solar system!
Titania is named after the queen of the fairies in Shakespeare's play 'A Midsummer Night's Dream.'
Titania has huge canyons that stretch for hundreds of miles across its icy surface!
Because Uranus is tilted on its side, Titania's orbit is tilted too. It goes over and under Uranus instead of around its middle!
Titania is made of about half ice and half rock. It's like a dirty snowball floating in space!

oberon.name: Oberon

stats.sizeVsEarth: Less than half the size of Earth's Moon

facts: Oberon is the second-largest moon of Uranus and the farthest major moon from the planet.
Oberon is named after the king of the fairies in Shakespeare's play — he's Titania's husband!
Oberon has a mysterious dark stuff on the floors of its craters. Scientists aren't sure what it is!
Oberon's surface is very old and covered in craters. It hasn't changed much in billions of years.
One of Oberon's craters has a mountain in the middle that's about 4 miles tall!

triton.name: Triton

stats.sizeVsEarth: A bit smaller than Earth's Moon

facts: Triton orbits Neptune backwards! It's the only large moon in the solar system that orbits in the opposite direction of its planet's spin.
Triton is one of the coldest places in the solar system. It's about minus 390 degrees! That's colder than Pluto!
Scientists think Triton didn't start as Neptune's moon — Neptune captured it from the Kuiper Belt, like catching a ball thrown from far away!
Triton has geysers that shoot dark stuff 5 miles into the sky! They're powered by the Sun warming frozen nitrogen.
Triton's surface looks like the skin of a cantaloupe melon — bumpy and wrinkled in a pattern scientists call 'cantaloupe terrain'!
Triton is slowly spiraling closer to Neptune. In billions of years, Neptune's gravity will tear it apart into a ring!

charon.name: Charon

stats.sizeVsEarth: About half the size of Pluto

facts: Charon is so big compared to Pluto that they spin around each other like two ice skaters holding hands!
Pluto and Charon always show the same face to each other. If you stood on Pluto, Charon would hang in one spot in the sky and never move!
Charon has a reddish-brown cap on its north pole. Scientists think it's made of gases that escaped from Pluto and got trapped there!
Charon has a huge canyon that's longer and deeper than the Grand Canyon on Earth!
Charon is named after the ferryman in Greek myths who carried souls across the river to the underworld. Fitting for Pluto's biggest moon!
